coredata.dc:title: Synthesis and application of nitrogen-doped carbon dots as a matrix in matrix-assisted laser desorption/ionization time-of-flight mass spectrometry
coredata.dc:creator: Maleki, Somayeh
Lee, Dabin
Kim, Yeoseon
Kim, Jeongkwon
coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were synthesized by microwave-assisted pyrolysis of citric acid and urea for use as an alternative matrix for the detection of small molecules using matrix-assisted laser desorption/ionization spectrometry (MALDI-MS). The synthesis of N-CDs is much simpler than that of other nitrogen-doped carbon matrices used in MALDI-MS, which has been a major limitation to its application. The synthesized N-CDs were characterized using various analytical methods. The high optical absorbance and good dispersibility of the obtained N-CDs showed them to be an ideal matrix for detecting analytes such as glucose, sucrose, amino acids, nilotinib, and polyethylene glycols. The application of N-CDs as a MALDI matrix improved the signal response of small molecules due to the specific aromatic structure and the doping of nitrogen atoms. The minimum detectable amounts using an N-CDs matrix in MALDI-MS were found to be in the range of 1 nmol–1 pmol, with a lower minimum detectable amount of arginine (1 pmol) than previously reported.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots
Matrix
Citric acid
Urea
MALDI-MS

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: MALDI-MS analysis using N-CDs as matrix
MALDI-MS analysis of sucrose and glucose using N-CDs as matrix
MALDI-MS analysis of amino acids, peptides, and proteins using N-CDs as matrix
MALDI-MS analysis of nilotinib using N-CDs as a matrix
MALDI-MS analysis of PEGs using N-CDs as matrix
Comparison of the minimum detectable amounts with other matrices

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea funded by the Ministry of Education ( 2016R1D1A1B02008854 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: MALDI mass spectra of (a) DHB only, (b) N-CDs only, (c) 100 nmol sucrose with N-CDs matrix and (d) 100 nmol glucose with N-CDs matrix.
MALDI mass spectra of (a) 100 nmol arginine, (b) 100 nmol lysine, (c) 100 nmol proline and (d) 100 nmol cysteine with N-CDs matrix.
MALDI mass spectra of 100 nmol nilotinib with N-CDs matrix.
MALDI mass spectra of (a) 100 nmol polyethylene glycol (PEG) 400, (b) 100 nmol PEG 1450, and (c) 10 nmol PEG 3350 with N-CDs matrix. (● indicates the peaks corresponding to [PEG + K]+).
Comparison of the minimum detectable amounts for MALDI-MS analysis of various samples using an N-CDs matrix with those of other matrices reported in other studies.

row.entry: 3 pmol using carbon nanotube in (+) mode [39]
33 pmol using sodiated DHB in (+) mode [39]
33 pmol using ionic liquid matrix (DHB-pyridine) in (+) mode [39]
500 pmol using binary matrix (DHB-aminopyrazine) in (+) mode [39]
500 pmol using gold nanoparticle in (+) mode [39]
1 nmol using wood charcoal in (+) mode [40]
10 fmol using carbon nanodots in (+) mode [4]
3 pmol using carbon nanotube in (+) mode [39]
33 pmol using ionic liquid matrix (DHB-pyridine) in (+) mode [39]
50 pmol using gold nanoparticle in (+) mode [39]
100 pmol using wood charcoal in (+) mode [40]
5 nmol using binary matrix (DHB-aminopyrazine) in (+) mode [39]
8 pmol using modified Mesoporous silica in (+) mode [41]
100 pmol using wood charcoal in (+) mode [40]
1 pmol using N-doped graphene in (−) mode [21]
3 pmol using sliver nanoparticles of CHCA matrix in (+) mode [42]
1 nmol using wood charcoal in (+) mode [40]
1 pmol using wood charcoal in (+) mode [40]
10 pmol using wood charcoal in (+) mode [40]

head.ce:title: Synthesis and application of nitrogen-doped carbon dots as a matrix in matrix-assisted laser desorption/ionization time-of-flight mass spectrometry

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Chemistry, Bu-Ali Sina University, Hamedan, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Chemistry, Bu-Ali Sina University, Hamedan, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Chungnam National University, Daejeon, 34134, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Chungnam National University, Daejeon, 34134, Republic of Korea

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Chemistry, Chungnam National University 99 Daehak-Ro, Yuseong-Gu, Daejeon, 34134, South Korea.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this study.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were synthesized by microwave-assisted pyrolysis of citric acid and urea for use as an alternative matrix for the detection of small molecules using matrix-assisted laser desorption/ionization spectrometry (MALDI-MS). The synthesis of N-CDs is much simpler than that of other nitrogen-doped carbon matrices used in MALDI-MS, which has been a major limitation to its application. The synthesized N-CDs were characterized using various analytical methods. The high optical absorbance and good dispersibility of the obtained N-CDs showed them to be an ideal matrix for detecting analytes such as glucose, sucrose, amino acids, nilotinib, and polyethylene glycols. The application of N-CDs as a MALDI matrix improved the signal response of small molecules due to the specific aromatic structure and the doping of nitrogen atoms. The minimum detectable amounts using an N-CDs matrix in MALDI-MS were found to be in the range of 1 nmol–1 pmol, with a lower minimum detectable amount of arginine (1 pmol) than previously reported.

ce:section.ce:para: Matrix-assisted laser desorption/ionization (MALDI) is a simple and useful soft ionization method. The MALDI matrix aids in the ionization of analytes and typically consists of an organic compound, such as 2,5-dihydroxybenzoic acid, α-cyano-4-hydroxycinnamic acid, and sinapic acid. Although organic matrices are effective for analyzing large molecules, such as proteins and peptides, in MALDI-mass spectrometry (MALDI-MS), they are not suitable for small molecule analysis due to strong background interference from intrinsic matrix-related ions in the low mass region [1,2]. Therefore, the development of new MALDI matrices for small molecule analysis is required.

Carbon dots (CDs) have recently been suggested as an alternative matrix for MALDI-MS [3–5]. CDs are usually less than 10 nm in size. Oxygen-containing functional groups, such as hydroxyl, carbonyl, and carboxylic groups, are present on the surface of the CDs. Since the discovery of fluorescent fragments in a batch of single-wall nanotubes, many studies investigating the synthesis, properties, and applications of CDs have been conducted [6,7]. CDs have come under increasing attention in bioimaging, chemical/biosensing, and photo/electrocatalysis applications due to their excellent physicochemical properties [3,8]. Unlike the conventional semiconductor quantum dots, which contain toxic heavy metal elements, CDs are mostly composed of non-toxic C, O, and N elements and possess a number of superior properties regarding their solubility in water, chemical inertness, photoluminescence, biocompatibility, and ease of functionalization. In addition, simple and cheap preparation methods along with high surface-to-volume ratios are additional advantages when considering the application of CDs [3–5,8,9].

The application of CDs as a matrix in MALDI-MS has several unique advantages, such as low background interference in the low m/z range and excellent water solubility, and analytes readily desorb from their surfaces [4]. In addition, the high ultraviolet light absorption efficiency of CDs makes them an ideal candidate as a matrix for detecting low molecular weight biomarkers in MALDI-MS. As the energy absorbed by CDs coincides with the N2 laser frequently used in MALDI-MS to ionize and desorb analytes, sufficient laser energy can be efficiently transferred to analytes, making CDs a superior matrix over conventional matrices [3,5]. Other carbon-based MALDI-MS matrices such as carbon nanotubes (CNT) [10], graphitized carbon black, graphene paper [11], graphite plates [12,13], and graphene sheets [14,15] have lower anisotropic nature and stability under high vacuum compared to the CDs. Due to these advantages, CDs have been applied as a MALDI matrix for detecting drugs [3]. In an effort to enhance MALDI performance, studies have modified the carbon-based matrix materials. Modifications previously investigated have included oxidation [16,17], acidification [18], immobilization [19], and combination [20] processing of the carbon-based matrices; however, adjusting the electronic properties of carbon materials by doping with nitrogen atoms, which have lone electron pairs, has been shown to result in the most obvious improvement in charge transfer performance [5,21,22]. N-doped graphene (NG) [21] and bulk graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) [23] have been successfully used as a matrix for MALDI-MS analysis; however, synthesis of such materials is difficult and considered a major limitation. Further investigations into other N-doped carbon materials with a simpler synthesis route for use as a matrix for MALDI-MS analysis are therefore required. Recently, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were synthesized using microwave-assisted pyrolysis of DL-malic acid and ethanolamine and were introduced as a matrix for the MALDI-MS analysis of hydroxy-polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons. Good dispersibility and a free matrix background, especially for negative ion mode analysis, are but a few of the significant advantages offered by the N-CDs matrix [5].

The application of N-CDs synthesized by simple microwave-assisted pyrolysis of citric acid and urea as a matrix for the analysis of small molecules in positive ion MALDI-MS is reported in this work. Citric acid and urea have been used previously to synthesize N-CDs in a microwave oven for 5 min [24–27] or in a Teflon autoclave at high temperatures for 6 h [28]. However, the application of N-CDs synthesized using citric acid and urea as a MALDI matrix has never been investigated.

Citric acid, urea, glucose, sucrose, polyethylene glycols (PEGs), L-cysteine, DL-proline, 2,5-dihydroxybenzoic acid (DHB), and sodium chloride were procured from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). L-arginine was procured from Alfa Aesar (Lancashire, UK). L-lysine was procured from ACROS Organics (Morris Plains, NJ, USA). Nilotinib was procured form MedChem Express (Monmouth Junction, NJ, USA).

Transmission electronic microscopy (TEM, Jeol 2100f 200 kV) was used to characterize the morphology, structure and size of synthesized N-CDs. An X-ray diffractometer (XRD, D8 ADVANCE X-ray Diffractometer) was used to determine the crystal structure of the N-CDs. An Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer (NICOLET iS10_smart iTR diamond ATR) was employed to measure the mid-infrared spectrum (500–4000 cm−1) of the N-CDs. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using a MultiLab 2000 spectrometer (Thermo Electron Corporation, Rugby, England). The fluorescence emission spectra were measured using a FS-2 (Scinco, Seoul, Korea). The UV–Visible spectrum (200–600 nm) was measured using an Optizen POP UV–Visible absorption spectrophotometer (Mecasys, Daejeon, Korea). MALDI-MS analysis was performed on a Voyager DE-STR (Applied Biosystems, Foster City, CA, USA).

N-CDs were synthesized by microwave-assisted pyrolysis of citric acid and urea in accordance with procedures reported in previous studies [24–27] with some modifications. Briefly, citric acid (1 g) and urea (1 g) were added to distilled water (10 mL). The solution was then heated in a domestic 800 W microwave oven for 4 min, during which the solution changed from a colorless liquid to a brown and finally to a dark brown clustered solid. The resulting brown solid was dissolved in water and centrifuged at 15,000 rpm for 15 min in order to remove large or agglomerated particles. The prepared brown solution was further purified using a 0.2-μm membrane filter. The flow-through solution was collected and used for characterization and application as a MALDI matrix. The obtained brown aqueous solution remained stable indefinitely.

To evaluate the performance of N-CDs as a MALDI matrix, various samples were analyzed, such as sucrose, glucose, arginine, cysteine, proline, lysine, PEG 400, PEG 1450, PEG 3350, and nilotinib. Water was used to dissolve all samples, with the exception of nilotinib, which is insoluble in water and was therefore dissolved in 88% formic acid [21]. The concentration of prepared sample solutions ranged from 1 pmol μL−1 to 100 nmol μL−1. The N-CDs matrix solution (10 mg mL−1) was prepared by dissolving the N-CDs in a 50% acetonitrile aqueous solution. The DHB matrix solution (10 mg mL−1) was prepared by dissolving 10 mg DHB in 1 mL of 50% acetonitrile and 1% phosphoric acid in water [29]. For the MALDI-MS analysis, the N-CDs matrix solution (1 μL) was loaded onto a MALDI plate. The sample (1 μL) was then loaded onto the dried matrix. After being allowed to dry at room temperature, the MALDI mass spectrum was subsequently obtained. In the case of sucrose and glucose, 1 μL of 100 mM NaCl was also loaded prior to MALDI MS measurements. The loading amount for each sample was 100 nmol, 10 nmol, 1 nmol, 100 pmol, 10 pmol, or 1 pmol. The MALDI mass spectra of N-CDs and DHB matrices were obtained by loading only each of the matrix on the MALDI plate. The MALDI mass spectra were obtained in positive ion reflection mode.
The synthesized N-CDs were characterized by FT-IR spectroscopy, TEM, XPS, XRD, UV–Visible absorption spectroscopy, and photoluminescence spectroscopy.

Supplementary Fig. S1 presents the FT-IR spectrum of the N-CDs. It shows a broad absorption band at around 3100–3500 cm−1 attributed to the presence N–H and O–H groups. The band at around 1635–1770 cm−1 is ascribed to the presence of surface C=O groups. The band around 1570 cm−1 is attributed to amide (CONH) bending. The bands around 1403–1363 cm−1 are from the stretching of C–N, and the bands around 1275–1182 cm−1 are from the vibration of C–O [8,24,25,27].

The UV–Visible (200–600 nm) absorption spectrum of N-CDs is shown in Supplementary Fig. S2. Typical absorptions of an aromatic pi system at 270 nm, 340 nm, and 405 nm [24] were observed from the N-CDs aqueous solution.

The emission spectrum (λ
ex = 420 nm) of the N-CDs is shown in Supplementary Fig. S3a. The maximum emission wavelength was observed at 522 nm. Excitation-dependent photoluminescence behavior was observed (see Supplementary Fig. S3b), which is common in fluorescent carbon materials. The excitation-dependent photoluminescence behavior can be attributed to optical selection of differently sized nanoparticles (quantum effect) and different emissive traps on the C-dot surface [30]. The XRD pattern of the N-CDs (see Supplementary Fig. S4) displayed a broad peak centered at 25°, which is attributed to highly disordered carbon atoms [24,31,32]. The TEM image of N-CDs (see Supplementary Fig. S5) shows they are predominantly spherical with an average diameter of approximately 4.0 nm.

The full scan XPS spectrum of N-CDs (see Supplementary Fig. S6a) shows three distinctive peaks at 288 eV, 402 eV, and 534 eV corresponding to C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s, respectively [25,28,33], indicating that the sample was doped with nitrogen from the urea. The high-resolution C 1s spectrum (see Supplementary Fig. S6b) shows a C–C/C=C peak with a binding energy of ∼284 eV, a C–N peak with a binding energy of ∼285 eV, a C=O peak with a binding energy of ∼287 eV, and an O–C=O (carboxylate group) peak with a binding energy of ∼288 eV [33,34]. The N 1s spectrum of the N-CDs (see Supplementary Fig. S6c) shows three peaks associated with pyridinic-N (398.3 eV), pyrrolic-N (399.2 eV), and quaternary nitrogen atoms (400.2 eV) [25,28,33,34].
When considering all of the analytical data described above, the N-CDs were considered to be successfully synthesized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: MALDI-MS analysis using N-CDs as matrix
MALDI-MS analysis of sucrose and glucose using N-CDs as matrix
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 presents the MALDI mass spectra of DHB matrix, N-CDs matrix, sucrose (MW = 342.3, C12H22O11) with N-CDs matrix, and glucose (MW = 180.2, C6H12O6) with N-CDs matrix. Significantly high background peaks were observed from the MALDI mass spectrum of DHB matrix (Fig. 1a), compared to the MALDI mass spectrum of N-CDs matrix (Fig. 1b). In the MALDI mass spectrum of sucrose (Fig. 1c), the peak at m/z 365.36 is assigned to [sucrose + Na]+. The peak at m/z 203.3 is assigned to [M+Na]+, where M is monosaccharide fragmented from sucrose [35]. In the MALDI mass spectrum of glucose (Fig. 1d), the peak at m/z 203.34 is assigned to [glucose + Na]+. The protonated form of glucose or sucrose, [M+H]+, was not observed. The minimum detectable amounts of sucrose and glucose were 100 pmol and 10 nmol, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: MALDI-MS analysis of amino acids, peptides, and proteins using N-CDs as matrix
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 presents the MALDI mass spectra of the amino acids arginine (MW = 174.2), cysteine (MW = 121.2), proline (MW = 115.1), and lysine (MW = 146.2) using N-CDs as the matrix. The amino acids were detected as [M+H]+ at m/z 175.4, m/z 122.3, m/z 116.3, and m/z 147.4, respectively. Peaks attributed to [M+Na]+ and [M+K]+ were also observed with reduced intensities, with the exception of cysteine, for which a [M+K]+ peak was not observed. The minimum detectable amounts of arginine, cysteine, proline, and lysine were 1 pmol, 100 nmol, 10 nmol, and 1 nmol, respectively.

Standard proteins, such as myoglobin or cytochrome c and its digested peptides, were also analyzed using N-CDs as a matrix. However, no peaks were observed in the MALDI mass spectra for these species, regardless of the quantities loaded onto the matrix.

ce:section.ce:section-title: MALDI-MS analysis of nilotinib using N-CDs as a matrix
ce:section.ce:para: Nilotinib is a tyrosine kinase inhibitor commonly used to treat chronic leukemia [36]. It is reported to be more effective than imatinib [37]. Nilotinib is not commonly detected by MALDI-MS due to its small size. N-doped graphene has been successfully used as a matrix to analyze nilotinib; 1 μM of niblotinib was detected in negative ion mode [21].

The N-CDs matrix was applied to analyze nilotinb by MALDI-MS. Fig. 3 shows the MALDI mass spectra of nilotinib (MW = 529.5, C28H22F3N7O1). Nilotinib was observed in its protonated, sodium adduct, and potassium adduct forms. The peak at m/z 530.4 was assigned to the protonated nilotinib form, [M+H]+. The peaks at m/z 552.4 and m/z 568.4 were assigned to the sodium adduct, [M+Na]+, and potassium adduct, [M+K]+, forms, respectively. The minimum detectable amount of nilotinib using N-CDs as MALDI matrix was 10 pmol.

ce:section.ce:section-title: MALDI-MS analysis of PEGs using N-CDs as matrix
ce:section.ce:para: PEG is a polyether compound having a formula of H(OCH2CH2)nOH, where n is the number of repeating units. The MALDI-MS analysis of PEGs generally exhibits a symmetrical distribution of multiple peaks with each peak differing by 44.02 Da mass units which is the mass of ethylene glycol [38]. Fig. 4 shows the MALDI mass spectra of PEG 400, PEG 1450, and PEG 3350 using N-CDs as MALDI matrix. A distinct polymer pattern of a symmetrical distribution was observed in all spectra. PEG 400, PEG 1450, and PEG 3350 were observed down to loadings of 1 nmol, 10 pmol, and 100 pmol, respectively. [PEG + Na]+ ion peaks were mainly observed in all the MALDI mass spectra of PEG 400, PEG 1450, and PEG 3350. Minor [PEG + K]+ ion peaks were observed in only the MALDI mass spectrum of PEG 400. In the MALDI mass spectrum of PEG 400, peaks of [PEG + Na]+ ions with n ranging from 5 to 19 and [PEG + K]+ ions with n ranging from 2 to 18 were observed, where the peaks of [PEG + Na]+ ion (n = 9) and [PEG + K]+ ion (n = 9) were observed at m/z 437.28 and m/z 453.20, respectively. In the MALDI mass spectrum of PEG 1450, [PEG + Na]+ ion peaks with n ranging from 10 to 51 were observed, where the peak at m/z 1449.84 corresponds to [H(OCH2CH2)32OH + Na]+ ion. In the MALDI mass spectrum of PEG 3350, peaks of polymer with n ranging from 13 to 99 were observed where the peak of [H(OCH2CH2)74OH + Na]+ was detected at m/z 3299.03.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of the minimum detectable amounts with other matrices
ce:section.ce:para: Table 1 compares the minimum detectable amounts of MALDI-MS analysis of various samples using an N-CD matrix reported in this study with those of other MALDI-MS studies previously reported. The minimum detectable amounts for the analysis of cysteine, proline, and lysine by other studies could not be found. The minimum detectable amounts in this study are higher than those previously reported, with the exception of arginine, which was at 1 pmol and lower than in other studies.

Previously, sucrose was detected in the levels of 3 pmol (carbon nanotubes), 33 pmol (sodiated DHB), 33 pmol (ionic liquid matrix), 500 pmol (binary matrix), 500 pmol (ZnO), and 500 pmol (gold nanoparticles) [39]. Using wood charcoal as a MALDI matrix [40], the lowest detectable level of sucrose was 1 nmol. The current detectable amount of 100 pmol sucrose is located in the middle of all the reports.

Glucose was reported to be detected in the level of 10 fmol (carbon nanodots) [4], 3 pmol (carbon nanotubes) [39], 33 pmol (ionic liquid matrix) [39], 50 pmol (ZnO) [39], 50 pmol (gold nanoparticles) [39], 100 pmol (wood charcoal) [40], 333 pmol (sodiated DHB) [39], and 5 nmol (binary matrix) [39]. The current detectable amount of 10 nmol glucose is highest among all the reports.

In the previous MALDI-MS analysis of arginine, 8 pmol or 100 pmol was necessary using mesoporous silica [41] or wood charcoal [40], respectively. Based on our literature search, the current requirement of 1 pmol arginine is the lowest detectable amount of arginine up to now in the MALDI-MS analysis of arginine.

No literature was found for the MALDI-MS analysis of cysteine, proline, and lysine. The interference in the low m/z region may have prevented analyzing these low molar mass compounds. The current detections will be the starting point for the MALDI-MS analysis of these compounds.

Only one publication was found for the MALDI MS analysis of nilotinib [21], where 1 pmol nilotinib was detected using N-doped graphene. In the current study, a little more amount of nilotinib (10 pmol) was needed for the MALDI-MS analysis.

PEGs were previously analyzed by MALDI MS using wood charcoal [40] or alpha-Cyano-4-hydroxycinnamic acid (CHCA) [42]. Using CHCA as a matrix, the detection limit of PEG400 was in the level of 3 pmol, which is significantly better than the current result of 1 nmol. Wood charcoal matrix was used to analyze PEG 400, PEG 1450 and PEG 3350 where the minimum detectable amounts were 1 nmol, 1 pmol, and 10 pmol, respectively, which are in general slightly better than the current results.

N-CDs were synthesized by microwave-assisted pyrolysis of citric acid and urea and characterized using a variety of techniques. The synthesized N-CDs were successfully applied as a matrix in positive ion mode MALDI-MS for the analysis of amino acids, a medicinal drug, and PEGs. For the First time, the N-CDs synthesized using citric acid and urea were applied as a MALDI matrix. When N-CDs were used as MALDI matrix, the minimum detectable amounts were found to be 100 pmol (sucrose), 10 nmol (glucose), 1 pmol (arginine), 100 nmol (cysteine), 10 nmol (proline), 1 nmol (lysine), 10 pmol (nilotinib), 1 nmol (PEG 400), 10 pmol (PEG 1450), and 100 pmol (PEG 3350).

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea funded by the Ministry of Education (2016R1D1A1B02008854).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijms.2019.05.004.
